  1. Reference
  2. Glossary

Glossary

Key terms and notation, with pointers to where each is developed

A compact reference for the central concepts of the book. Each entry links to the page where the idea is introduced and developed in full.

Foundations: Potential Outcomes

Potential outcome
The outcome a unit would take under a given treatment, written Y_i(1) (under treatment) and Y_i(0) (under control). Only one is ever observed. See Potential Outcomes.
Individual treatment effect (ITE)
The difference \tau_i = Y_i(1) - Y_i(0) for a single unit. Never directly observable — the Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference.
Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference
We observe each unit under only one treatment state, so the counterfactual outcome — and hence \tau_i — is missing for every unit. Causal inference is fundamentally a missing-data problem.
ATE / ATT / ATU
Average Treatment Effect E[Y(1)-Y(0)]; Average effect on the Treated E[Y(1)-Y(0)\mid D=1]; Average effect on the Untreated E[Y(1)-Y(0)\mid D=0]. They coincide only without selection on gains.
Switching equation
Y_i^{\text{obs}} = D_i Y_i(1) + (1-D_i) Y_i(0) — the rule mapping potential outcomes to the one we observe.
SUTVA
The Stable Unit Treatment Value Assumption: no interference between units, and a single version of treatment. Required for Y_i(d) to be well defined. See Potential Outcomes.
Selection bias
The component of a naive treatment–control comparison due to pre-existing differences: E[Y(0)\mid D=1] - E[Y(0)\mid D=0]. The decomposition Naive = ATT + selection bias is developed in Selection Bias.
Randomization
Random assignment of treatment, which makes D independent of the potential outcomes, eliminating selection bias in expectation and balancing observed and unobserved covariates. See Randomization.

Graphical Causal Models (DAGs)

DAG
Directed Acyclic Graph: nodes are variables, directed edges are direct causal effects, and no cycles are allowed. Encodes causal assumptions. See DAG Basics.
Confounder
A common cause of treatment and outcome (D \leftarrow U \rightarrow Y); leaving it uncontrolled opens a back-door path and biases the estimate.
Collider
A variable with two arrows pointing into it (D \rightarrow C \leftarrow Y). A collider blocks a path when left alone, and opens it when conditioned on — the source of collider (selection) bias. See Good vs Bad Controls.
Mediator
A variable on the causal pathway from treatment to outcome (D \rightarrow M \rightarrow Y). Controlling for it removes part of the very effect you want.
d-separation
The graphical criterion for conditional independence: two variables are d-separated given a set Z if Z blocks every path between them. See d-Separation.
Back-door criterion
A set Z identifies the causal effect of D on Y if it blocks all back-door paths and contains no descendants of D. The basis for valid covariate adjustment.

Statistical Foundations

Sampling distribution
The distribution of an estimator across hypothetical repeated samples. Its spread is the standard error. See Distributions & Spread.
Standard error
The standard deviation of an estimator’s sampling distribution; for a sample mean, \sigma/\sqrt{n}. Measures estimation uncertainty, and shrinks like 1/\sqrt{n}.
p-value
The probability, if the null were true, of a test statistic at least as extreme as the one observed. Not the probability the null is true. See Hypothesis Testing.
Type I / Type II error
Rejecting a true null (false positive, rate \alpha); failing to reject a false null (false negative, rate \beta). Power is 1-\beta.
Confidence interval
An interval constructed so that, across repeated samples, a fraction 1-\alpha of such intervals contain the true parameter. See Confidence Intervals.
CI–test duality
A value \theta_0 lies inside the (1-\alpha) confidence interval iff the two-sided level-\alpha test fails to reject \theta_0.

Regression

OLS
Ordinary Least Squares: the coefficients minimizing the sum of squared residuals; \hat\beta = (X'X)^{-1}X'y. Fitted values are the projection of y onto the column space of X. See OLS Mechanics.
Frisch–Waugh–Lovell (FWL)
A multiple-regression coefficient equals the simple-regression coefficient on the residualized regressor — “controlling for” means “partialling out.” See Frisch-Waugh-Lovell.
Omitted variable bias (OVB)
The bias from excluding a relevant correlated regressor: (effect of the omitted variable on Y) \times (regression of the omitted variable on the included one). See Omitted Variable Bias.
Interaction term
A product regressor x \cdot g allowing the effect of x to depend on g (effect heterogeneity). See Interactions.
Heteroskedasticity
Non-constant error variance. Leaves OLS unbiased but makes classical standard errors wrong; use robust (HC) standard errors. See Inference.
Clustered standard errors
Standard errors that allow arbitrary correlation within groups (clusters), used when observations are dependent within units/regions.

Panel Data & Difference-in-Differences

Panel data
Repeated observations on the same units over time, y_{it}. Enables controlling for time-invariant unobserved heterogeneity. See Panel Structure.
Fixed effects
Unit-specific intercepts \alpha_i absorbed by the within (demeaning) transformation, removing all time-invariant confounders. See Fixed Effects.
Difference-in-differences (DiD)
Comparing the change in outcomes for a treated group to the change for a control group: (\bar Y^{\text{post}}_T - \bar Y^{\text{pre}}_T) - (\bar Y^{\text{post}}_C - \bar Y^{\text{pre}}_C). See Classic 2x2 DiD.
Parallel trends
The DiD identifying assumption: absent treatment, treated and control groups would have followed the same outcome trend. Untestable, but probed with pre-trends. See Parallel Trends.
Event study
A specification with treatment leads and lags; pre-period coefficients near zero support parallel trends, post-period coefficients trace dynamic effects. See Diagnostics.
Two-way fixed effects (TWFE)
A regression with unit and time fixed effects plus a treatment indicator. A valid DiD with common timing, but biased under staggered adoption with heterogeneous effects. See Staggered Adoption.
Goodman–Bacon decomposition
Shows TWFE is a weighted average of all 2x2 DiDs, including “forbidden” comparisons using already-treated units as controls — the source of negative weights under staggered timing.

Instrumental Variables & Regression Discontinuity

Endogeneity
\operatorname{Cov}(x,\varepsilon)\neq 0, from confounding, simultaneity, or measurement error, which makes OLS inconsistent. See The Endogeneity Problem.
Instrument
A variable z that is relevant (predicts x), satisfies the exclusion restriction (affects y only through x), and is exogenous. See Relevance and Exclusion.
Two-stage least squares (2SLS)
The IV estimator \hat\beta_{IV} = \operatorname{Cov}(z,y)/\operatorname{Cov}(z,x) — the reduced form divided by the first stage. See Two-Stage Least Squares.
Weak instrument
An instrument with a small first stage (low F), causing severe bias toward OLS and a non-normal, high-variance IV distribution. See Weak Instruments.
LATE
Local Average Treatment Effect: with a binary instrument and monotonicity, IV identifies the effect for compliers only, not the ATE. See LATE and Compliers.
Regression discontinuity (RD)
A design exploiting a treatment that switches at a cutoff in a running variable; the jump in the outcome at the cutoff identifies a local effect. See Sharp RD.
Fuzzy RD
An RD where crossing the cutoff changes the probability of treatment; the estimator is the outcome jump over the treatment jump — an IV/Wald ratio. See Fuzzy RD.
McCrary density test
A falsification test for manipulation of the running variable: a discontinuity in its density at the cutoff signals sorting. See Validity and Falsification.

Selection on Observables & Synthetic Control

Conditional independence (unconfoundedness)
(Y(0),Y(1)) \perp D \mid X: treatment is as good as random given observed covariates. The key assumption behind matching and weighting. See Selection on Observables.
Overlap (common support)
0 < P(D=1\mid X) < 1 for all X: treated and control units exist at every covariate value. Required for adjustment to identify effects. See Overlap and Balance Diagnostics.
Propensity score
e(X) = P(D=1\mid X). Conditioning on this scalar balancing score suffices to remove bias under unconfoundedness. See Propensity Scores.
Inverse-probability weighting (IPW)
Reweighting units by the inverse of their treatment probability to build a balanced pseudo-population. See Weighting and Doubly Robust Estimation.
Doubly robust estimation
Combining an outcome model and a propensity model so the estimator is consistent if either is correctly specified (AIPW).
Standardized mean difference
A scale-free measure of covariate imbalance between groups (rule of thumb: below 0.1 is good balance), the right diagnostic after matching/weighting.
Synthetic control
A counterfactual built as a weighted average of untreated donor units that tracks the treated unit’s pre-treatment path; the post-treatment gap is the effect. See The Synthetic Control Method.
Placebo (permutation) test
Inference for synthetic control: apply the method to each donor as if treated, and judge whether the treated unit’s gap (or RMSPE ratio) is unusual relative to the placebo distribution. See Inference for Synthetic Control.
Bayesian Regression and Hierarchical Models
